name: Asopus
description: Asopus is a river god in Greek mythology, the son of Oceanus and Tethys, or Poseidon and Pero, or even Zeus and Eurynome, depending on the source. He is the personification of the river of the same name in Boeotia, Greece. Asopus is primarily known for his numerous daughters, many of whom were seduced by gods like Zeus and Apollo. His most famous daughter is Aegina, who was abducted by Zeus, prompting Asopus to pursue the god. This pursuit ended when Zeus struck him down with a thunderbolt, causing him to return to his riverbed with a permanent limp, symbolized by the river's winding course. Asopus is considered a protector of his river and its surrounding lands, providing fresh water and fertility. He is often depicted as a muscular, bearded man reclining against an urn or holding a reed, representing the river's abundance. His lineage and the stories surrounding his daughters highlight the complex relationships between gods and mortals in Greek mythology and the consequences of divine intervention in human affairs. He represents the power and life-giving properties of fresh water, but also the vulnerability of rivers to divine wrath. His story underscores the interconnectedness of the natural world and the divine realm.
subtitle: River God of Boeotia

keyMyths.title: Abduction of Aegina
keyMyths.description: Zeus abducted Asopus' daughter Aegina, leading Asopus to pursue him. Zeus struck Asopus down with a thunderbolt, causing him to limp.

keyMyths.title: Offspring of Asopus
keyMyths.description: Asopus fathered numerous daughters, many of whom were seduced by gods. These daughters became associated with various cities and springs.

keyMyths.title: Asopus' Lineage
keyMyths.description: Various accounts exist regarding Asopus' parentage, some claiming Oceanus and Tethys, others Poseidon and Pero, or even Zeus and Eurynome.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
